text: Chapter 4 builds the book's central thesis that the fretboard is a matrix to be visualized chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases flowing out of chord knowledge organized into four root-string zones (6th, 5th, 4th, and 3rd strings, the last only for triads). The chord universe is partitioned into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony, the superimposition of thirds (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). Under Triads, the chapter gives interval formulas for the four qualities, names the three inversion positions (root, first, second), introduces slash-chord notation (e.g., C/E), and presents the book's signature 'Chord Skeletons' - pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants, plus playability caveats (e.g., avoid the 6th-string left-side minor shape) and the rule that diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric. Tetrad Chords expand to Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework, the convention that an unmarked 7 is minor (major 7 must be indicated), enharmonic equivalences (C6 = Am7/first-inversion; Cm(b6) = Abmaj7/first-inversion), and Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations restricted to tetrads. Voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule, and characteristic voicing prohibitions appear - notably avoiding a flat-9 between bass and top note on major/minor chords. Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4), and the chapter recommends visualizing them by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes, decoding jazz symbols like Gsus9 and Gsus13 into precise tone content. Extended Chords cover 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions, codifying the #11-vs-natural-11 rule (use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely), defining altered chords as dominants with more than one altered extension, and noting that the 5th is almost always omitted in 13th chords. The chapter also maps 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes (typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord) and surfaces the dominant-7 web of enharmonic relationships with diminished, half-diminished, and maj7(#4) chords. A load-bearing instrument constraint runs throughout: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes, which drives nearly every voicing choice in the chapter.
